1:
"Now of the things which we have spoken this is the sum: We have such an high priest, who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens;" The witnesses note the sum of the argument: we have an enthroned high priest in heaven. On "of the things which we have spoken this is the sum," the grammatical voices note the summary. Barnes and Robertson note the writer gathering up his argument: "of the things which we have spoken this is the sum (kephalaion — the chief point, the crowning summary)" — the central point of all that has been said about the priesthood is now stated.

On "we have such an high priest," the witnesses note the possession. Barnes and Calvin note the emphasis: "we have (now possess) such an high priest" — believers actually have a high priest of the surpassing dignity just described (the Melchizedek priest, ch. 7); he is ours.

On "set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the enthronement: he "is set (sat down, enthroned) on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty (God) in the heavens" — unlike the earthly priests who stood to minister, our high priest is seated, enthroned at God's right hand in heaven; his finished work and royal session. The verse displays the sum of the argument: an enthroned high priest in heaven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summary — "of the things which we have spoken this is the sum (the chief point)," gathering up the argument (Barnes, Robertson); "we have (possess) such an high priest," believers actually having the surpassing priest described (Barnes, Calvin); "set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens," enthroned at God's right hand, his finished work and royal session (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enthroned priest. The verse marks the summary: this is the sum. The verse displays the possession: we have such a priest. The verse displays the enthronement: at God's right hand. The verse displays the place: in the heavens. The summary displays our high priest enthroned in heaven. The verse displays the priest: ours. The verse displays the session: seated. The verse displays the Majesty: God. The sum of the argument — an enthroned high priest in heaven — displays the crowning point of the whole discussion: we have such a high priest, of the surpassing Melchizedek dignity, who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens. The verse displays the heavenly high priest, minister of the true sanctuary (v.2).
2:
"A minister of the sanctuary, and of the true tabernacle, which the Lord pitched, and not man." The brief verse notes Christ a minister of the true heavenly tabernacle. The grammatical voices note the heavenly ministry. Barnes and Robertson note Christ's sphere of service: "a minister (leitourgos — one who performs sacred service) of the sanctuary (ta hagia — the holy place), and of the true (alethinos — real, genuine, as opposed to the typical) tabernacle" — Christ ministers not in the earthly tent but in the true, heavenly sanctuary.

On "the true tabernacle," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note that the "true tabernacle" is heaven itself (or the heavenly sanctuary), of which the earthly was only a copy; Christ's ministry is in the reality, not the shadow.

On "which the Lord pitched, and not man," the grammatical-historical note the divine author: this true tabernacle "the Lord pitched (set up, erected), and not man" — unlike the Mosaic tabernacle pitched by human hands, the heavenly sanctuary is God's own making; its divine origin marks its superiority. The verse displays Christ a minister of the true heavenly tabernacle.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's sphere — "a minister (one who performs sacred service) of the sanctuary, and of the true (real) tabernacle," ministering in the true heavenly sanctuary not the earthly tent (Barnes, Robertson); "the true tabernacle" heaven itself, of which the earthly was a copy, the reality not the shadow (Barnes, Calvin); "which the Lord pitched, and not man," God's own making, not human hands, its divine origin marking its superiority (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly minister. The verse marks the ministry: of the sanctuary. The verse displays the tabernacle: the true. The verse displays the contrast: real, not typical. The verse displays the author: the Lord, not man. The ministry displays Christ serving in the true sanctuary. The verse displays the minister: Christ. The verse displays the tabernacle: heavenly. The verse displays the pitching: by God. Christ a minister of the true heavenly tabernacle displays the sphere of his priestly service: he ministers not in the earthly tent but in the sanctuary and true tabernacle — the real, heavenly sanctuary which the Lord himself, not man, pitched. The verse displays the heavenly ministry, which requires somewhat to offer (v.3).
3:
"For every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices: wherefore it is of necessity that this man have somewhat also to offer." The brief verse notes that Christ, as high priest, must have somewhat to offer. The grammatical voices note the priestly function. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "every high priest is ordained (appointed, constituted) to offer gifts and sacrifices" — the essential office of a high priest is to offer; this is the very purpose of his appointment (cf. 5:1).

On "it is of necessity that this man have somewhat also to offer," the witnesses note the conclusion. Barnes and Calvin note the inference: "it is of necessity (necessary) that this man (Christ) have somewhat also to offer" — since every high priest must offer, Christ too, being a high priest, must have an offering; he could not be a priest without a sacrifice to present.

On the offering, the grammatical-historical note the unique sacrifice: Christ's "somewhat to offer" is, as the epistle shows, himself — his own blood (9:12, 14); unlike the earthly priests who offered animals, Christ offered his own person; the necessity of an offering points to his self-sacrifice. The verse displays that Christ must have somewhat to offer.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "every high priest is ordained (appointed) to offer gifts and sacrifices," offering the essential office (Barnes, Robertson); "it is of necessity that this man (Christ) have somewhat also to offer," Christ too needing an offering, unable to be priest without a sacrifice (Barnes, Calvin); Christ's offering being himself, his own blood, the unique self-sacrifice (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the necessary offering. The verse marks the function: to offer gifts. The verse displays the necessity: somewhat to offer. The verse displays the priest: Christ. The verse displays the offering: himself. The principle displays Christ the priest with his own sacrifice. The verse displays the offering: required. The verse displays the necessity: certain. The verse displays the sacrifice: his own. That Christ, as high priest, must have somewhat to offer displays the requirement of his office: since every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices, Christ too must have an offering — which, as the epistle shows, is himself, his own blood. The verse displays the necessary sacrifice, which could not be the earthly gifts (v.4).
4:
"For if he were on earth, he should not be a priest, seeing that there are priests that offer gifts according to the law:" The brief verse notes that Christ's priesthood is not earthly but heavenly. The grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note the reasoning: "if he were on earth, he should not be a priest (he would not even be a priest at all)" — Christ's priesthood is not of the earthly, Levitical kind; on earth he would not qualify, being of Judah, not Levi (7:14).

On "seeing that there are priests that offer gifts according to the law," the witnesses note the existing order. Barnes and Calvin note the reason: "there are (already) priests that offer gifts according to the law" — the earthly priestly office was fully occupied by the Levitical priests, appointed by the law; there was no room or need for another earthly priest.

On the point, the grammatical-historical note the heavenly sphere: Christ's priesthood belongs not to earth but to heaven — he ministers in the true tabernacle above (v.2); his office is of a different and higher order than the earthly Levitical one, which the law already provided. The verse displays that Christ's priesthood is heavenly, not earthly.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reasoning — "if he were on earth, he should not be a priest," Christ not qualifying for the earthly Levitical priesthood, being of Judah (7:14, Barnes, Robertson); "there are priests that offer gifts according to the law," the earthly office fully occupied by the Levites, no room for another (Barnes, Calvin); Christ's priesthood belonging to heaven, the true tabernacle, of a higher order (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heavenly priesthood. The verse marks the negative: not a priest on earth. The verse displays the reason: priests already by law. The verse displays the sphere: heavenly. The verse displays the order: higher. The argument displays Christ's priesthood above the earthly. The verse displays the earth: not his sphere. The verse displays the priests: Levitical. The verse displays the heaven: his ministry. That Christ's priesthood is heavenly, not earthly, displays the place of his office: on earth he would not even be a priest, for the earthly priesthood was already filled by the Levites appointed by law — his priesthood is of a higher, heavenly order, exercised in the true tabernacle above. The verse displays the heavenly priest, the earthly priests serving but a shadow (v.5).
5:
"Who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount." The witnesses note the earthly priests serving a shadow of heavenly things. On "who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things," the grammatical voices note the typical service. Barnes and Robertson note the nature of the Levitical service: "who serve unto the example (hypodeigma — a copy, sketch, representation) and shadow (skia — a faint outline) of heavenly things" — the earthly priests minister at a copy and shadow of the true, heavenly realities; their service is figurative, pointing beyond itself.

On "as Moses was admonished of God," the witnesses note the divine pattern. Barnes and Calvin note the proof: "as Moses was admonished (warned, divinely instructed) of God when he was about to make the tabernacle" — God himself gave Moses a pattern; the earthly tabernacle was made after a heavenly model.

On "make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount," the grammatical-historical note the heavenly original (Exodus 25:40): "make all things according to the pattern (typos — model, archetype) shewed to thee in the mount" — Moses was to copy exactly the pattern shown him on Sinai; the very command proves that the earthly was a copy of a heavenly original, confirming that Christ's heavenly ministry is the reality the earthly shadowed. The verse displays the earthly priests serving a shadow of heavenly things.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the typical service — "who serve unto the example (copy) and shadow (faint outline) of heavenly things," the earthly priests ministering at a copy of the heavenly realities, figurative service (Barnes, Robertson); "Moses was admonished of God," God giving the pattern, the tabernacle made after a heavenly model (Barnes, Calvin); "make all things according to the pattern shewed thee in the mount" (Exodus 25:40), Moses copying the heavenly original, proving the earthly a copy (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the shadow-service. The verse marks the service: the copy and shadow. The verse displays the realities: heavenly things. The verse displays the pattern: shown to Moses. The verse displays the original: the heavenly. The service displays the earthly priests ministering a copy of heaven. The verse displays the shadow: served. The verse displays the pattern: divine. The verse displays the mount: where shown. The earthly priests serving a shadow of heavenly things displays the typical nature of the old order: they minister at a mere copy and shadow of the heavenly realities — as proved by God's command that Moses make the tabernacle exactly after the pattern shown him in the mount, the earthly being a copy of the heavenly. The verse displays the shadow and the reality, grounding Christ's more excellent ministry (v.6).
6:
"But now hath he obtained a more excellent ministry, by how much also he is the mediator of a better covenant, which was established upon better promises." The witnesses note Christ's more excellent ministry as mediator of a better covenant. On "now hath he obtained a more excellent ministry," the grammatical voices note the superior service. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "now hath he obtained (attained, received) a more excellent (superior, surpassing) ministry" — Christ's priestly ministry is far superior to the Levitical, as the heavenly reality surpasses the earthly shadow.

On "he is the mediator of a better covenant," the witnesses note the mediatorship. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of the superiority: "by how much (in proportion as) also he is the mediator (mesites — the go-between, one who acts between two parties to reconcile) of a better covenant (diatheke)" — Christ mediates a covenant better than the old; his ministry is superior because the covenant he mediates is superior.

On "established upon better promises," the puritan Henry notes the better promises: the better covenant "was established (enacted, legally founded) upon better promises" — promises better than those of the old covenant; the new covenant rests on richer promises (forgiveness, the law written on the heart, the knowledge of God, vv.10-12). The verse displays Christ's more excellent ministry as mediator of a better covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the superior service — "now hath he obtained a more excellent (surpassing) ministry," Christ's ministry far superior to the Levitical (Barnes, Robertson); "he is the mediator (go-between) of a better covenant," his ministry superior because the covenant is superior (Barnes, Calvin); "established upon better promises," the new covenant resting on richer promises (Henry). The verse displays the better covenant. The verse marks the ministry: more excellent. The verse displays the mediator: of a better covenant. The verse displays the covenant: better. The verse displays the foundation: better promises. The ministry displays Christ mediating the superior covenant. The verse displays the ministry: surpassing. The verse displays the mediator: Christ. The verse displays the promises: better. Christ's more excellent ministry as mediator of a better covenant displays the superiority of his priesthood: he has obtained a ministry far surpassing the Levitical, in proportion as he is the mediator of a better covenant — one established upon better promises than the old. The verse displays the better covenant, needed because the first was faulty (v.7).
7:
"For if that first covenant had been faultless, then should no place have been sought for the second." The brief verse notes that the first covenant's fault required a second. The grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note the reasoning: "if that first covenant had been faultless (amemptos — without defect, perfect), then should no place have been sought for the second" — had the old covenant been perfect, accomplishing all that was needed, there would have been no occasion for a new one; the very promise of a second proves the first deficient.

On "faultless," the witnesses note the defect. Barnes and Calvin note that the first covenant was not "faultless" — not in itself sinful, but defective, weak, unable to perfect (cf. 7:18-19); its fault lay in its inability to take away sin and bring men fully to God.

On "no place... for the second," the puritan Henry notes the inference: that God sought "place for the second" (introduced a new covenant) proves the first was insufficient; the announcement of a new covenant (Jeremiah 31, v.8) is itself the verdict on the old. The verse displays that the first covenant's fault required a second.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reasoning — "if that first covenant had been faultless (perfect), no place would have been sought for the second," the promise of a new one proving the first deficient (Barnes, Robertson); the first not "faultless," defective and weak, unable to perfect (7:18-19, Barnes, Calvin); God's seeking place for a second proving the first insufficient (Henry). The verse displays the faulty first covenant. The verse marks the test: if faultless. The verse displays the defect: not perfect. The verse displays the proof: a second sought. The verse displays the verdict: the first insufficient. The argument displays the old covenant shown deficient by the new. The verse displays the first: faulty. The verse displays the second: needed. The verse displays the place: sought. That the first covenant's fault required a second displays the proof of the old covenant's deficiency: had it been faultless, no place would have been sought for a new one — but the very promise of a second covenant shows the first was weak and insufficient. The verse displays the faulty first covenant, replaced by the promised new (vv.8-12).
8:
"For finding fault with them, he saith, Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah:" The witnesses note God's promise of a new covenant, finding fault with the old. On "finding fault with them," the grammatical voices note the divine censure. Barnes and Robertson note that God himself found fault: "finding fault with them (the people, or the covenant)" — interpreters differ whether the fault is found with the people (who broke the covenant) or with the covenant itself; the Greek allows "with them," pointing to the people's failure under the old covenant, which revealed its weakness.

On "Behold, the days come... I will make a new covenant," the witnesses note the promise (Jeremiah 31:31-34). Barnes and Calvin note the great prophecy now quoted: "the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make (conclude, ratify) a new covenant (kaine diatheke — a new arrangement, fresh in kind)" — God promised, through Jeremiah, a new covenant to replace the old; the longest Old Testament quotation in the New.

On "with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah," the grammatical-historical note the recipients: the new covenant is "with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah" — originally with the divided nation, fulfilled in the spiritual Israel, the church; God's people receive the new covenant. The verse displays God's promise of a new covenant, finding fault with the old.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the censure — "finding fault with them (the people, or the covenant)," God finding fault, whether with the people who broke it or the covenant itself, revealing its weakness (Barnes, Robertson); the promise (Jeremiah 31:31-34) "the days come... I will make a new covenant," God promising a new covenant to replace the old (Barnes, Calvin); "with the house of Israel and Judah," the new covenant with God's people, fulfilled in the church (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the new-covenant promise. The verse marks the fault: found with them. The verse displays the promise: a new covenant. The verse displays the source: the Lord. The verse displays the recipients: Israel and Judah. The promise displays God announcing the new covenant. The verse displays the fault: found. The verse displays the covenant: new. The verse displays the days: coming. God's promise of a new covenant, finding fault with the old, displays the prophetic ground of the change: finding fault, God declared through Jeremiah that the days would come when he would make a new covenant with the house of Israel and Judah — the great promise the writer now expounds. The verse displays the new covenant promised, unlike the broken old one (v.9).
9:
"Not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt; because they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not, saith the Lord." The witnesses note the new covenant unlike the broken Sinai covenant. On "not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note that the new covenant differs from the old: "not according to (not like) the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt" — the new covenant is not a renewal of the Sinai covenant made at the Exodus but is different in kind.

On "I took them by the hand," the witnesses note the tenderness. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: God "took them by the hand" — like a father leading a child, a figure of tender care in the deliverance from Egypt; yet that covenant was broken.

On "they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the failure: "they continued not (did not abide) in my covenant, and I regarded them not (disregarded them, or turned away)" — the people broke the covenant by disobedience, and God, in consequence, withdrew his favour; the old covenant failed through human unfaithfulness, showing the need of a better. The verse displays the new covenant unlike the broken Sinai covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "not according to (not like) the covenant made with their fathers... led out of Egypt," the new covenant different in kind from the Sinai covenant (Barnes, Robertson); "I took them by the hand," God's tender care in the deliverance, yet the covenant broken (Barnes, Calvin); "they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not," the people breaking it by disobedience, God withdrawing favour, the old covenant failing through human unfaithfulness (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the broken covenant. The verse marks the contrast: not like the old. The verse displays the deliverance: out of Egypt. The verse displays the failure: they continued not. The verse displays the consequence: God regarded them not. The contrast displays the new covenant unlike the broken old. The verse displays the covenant: different. The verse displays the breaking: by the people. The verse displays the disregard: God's. The new covenant unlike the broken Sinai covenant displays why a new one was needed: it is not like the covenant made with the fathers at the Exodus, which they broke by disobedience so that God withdrew his favour — the old covenant's failure through human unfaithfulness showing the need of a better, inward one. The verse displays the broken old covenant, replaced by the inward new (v.10).
10:
"For this is the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, saith the Lord; I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts: and I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people:" The witnesses note the new covenant: the law written on the heart, God their God. On "I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts," the grammatical voices note the inward law. Barnes and Robertson note the first great promise of the new covenant: "I will put my laws into their mind (their understanding), and write them in their hearts (their inmost being)" — unlike the old covenant, written on tables of stone and external, the new covenant writes the law within, on the mind and heart; an inward, transforming law.

On the inward writing, the Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the regenerating work. Calvin and Cyril note that the law written on the heart is the work of the Spirit in regeneration — God inscribes his will inwardly, so that the believer obeys not by external compulsion but from a renewed heart; this is the great superiority of the new covenant, an inward principle of obedience.

On "I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people," the grammatical-historical note the covenant bond: "I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people" — the heart of the covenant relationship, the mutual belonging of God and his people; God owns them, and they are his. The verse displays the new covenant: the law written on the heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inward law — "I will put my laws into their mind, and write them in their hearts," the new covenant writing the law within, not on stone, an inward transforming law (Barnes, Robertson); the law on the heart the Spirit's regenerating work, obedience from a renewed heart (Calvin, Cyril); "I will be to them a God, and they shall be to me a people," the covenant bond, the mutual belonging of God and his people (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inward covenant. The verse marks the law: in the mind and heart. The verse displays the writing: inward. The verse displays the work: the Spirit's. The verse displays the bond: God and people. The covenant displays the law written within and the mutual belonging. The verse displays the laws: inscribed. The verse displays the hearts: written on. The verse displays the God: theirs. The new covenant — the law written on the heart, God their God — displays the great superiority of the new arrangement: God writes his law not on stone but within the mind and heart, by the Spirit's regenerating work, and binds himself to his people in mutual belonging — he their God, they his people. The verse displays the inward covenant, in which all shall know the Lord (v.11).
11:
"And they shall not teach every man his neighbour, and every man his brother, saying, Know the Lord: for all shall know me, from the least to the greatest." The brief verse notes the new covenant promise that all shall know the Lord. The grammatical voices note the universal knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note the second great promise: "they shall not teach every man his neighbour... saying, Know the Lord: for all shall know me, from the least to the greatest" — under the new covenant, the knowledge of God is not confined to a few teachers but is shared by all God's people; every member, high and low, knows the Lord.

On "all shall know me," the witnesses note the personal knowledge. Barnes and Calvin note that this is not merely external instruction but a real, inward, saving knowledge of God, given to all believers; the new covenant brings a personal acquaintance with God that the old, with its external teaching, did not universally impart.

On "from the least to the greatest," the grammatical-historical note the universality: this knowledge extends "from the least to the greatest" — to every member of the covenant people without exception; not the priests and learned only, but all, even the humblest, know God savingly. The verse displays the new covenant promise that all shall know the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second promise — "they shall not teach... Know the Lord: for all shall know me," the knowledge of God shared by all God's people, not confined to a few (Barnes, Robertson); not external instruction but real inward saving knowledge, a personal acquaintance with God (Barnes, Calvin); "from the least to the greatest," extending to every member without exception, even the humblest (grammatical-historical). The verse displays universal knowledge. The verse marks the teaching: no longer needed externally. The verse displays the knowledge: all shall know me. The verse displays the nature: inward, saving. The verse displays the scope: least to greatest. The promise displays all God's people knowing him. The verse displays the knowing: universal. The verse displays the Lord: known. The verse displays the all: from least to greatest. The new covenant promise that all shall know the Lord displays its second great blessing: under it the knowledge of God is no longer the preserve of a teaching few but is given to all the covenant people — from the least to the greatest, every member knows the Lord with a real, inward, saving knowledge. The verse displays the universal knowledge of God, grounded in the forgiveness of sins (v.12).
12:
"For I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more." The brief verse notes the new covenant promise of mercy and forgotten sins. The grammatical voices note the ground of the covenant. Barnes and Robertson note the crowning promise, the basis of all the rest: "I will be merciful to (gracious toward) their unrighteousness" — God's mercy toward the sins of his people is the foundation of the new covenant; forgiveness underlies the inward law and the knowledge of God.

On "their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more," the witnesses note the full pardon. Barnes and Calvin note the completeness: "their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more (will I no longer hold against them)" — God's forgiveness is so complete that he treats their sins as if forgotten, never bringing them up again; a full and final pardon, unlike the old covenant's yearly remembrance of sins (10:3).

On the connection, the grammatical-historical note the foundation: this forgiveness ("for") is the ground of the whole new covenant — because God forgives fully, he writes his law on the heart and gives the knowledge of himself; pardon is the root of the new covenant's blessings. The verse displays the new covenant promise of mercy and forgotten sins.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the crowning promise — "I will be merciful to (gracious toward) their unrighteousness," God's mercy the foundation of the new covenant (Barnes, Robertson); "their sins and iniquities will I remember no more," a forgiveness so complete sins are treated as forgotten, never raised again, unlike the old yearly remembrance (10:3, Barnes, Calvin); this forgiveness the ground of the whole covenant (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the forgiving covenant. The verse marks the mercy: to their unrighteousness. The verse displays the forgetting: sins remembered no more. The verse displays the completeness: full pardon. The verse displays the foundation: forgiveness underlies all. The promise displays God's full pardon grounding the covenant. The verse displays the mercy: shown. The verse displays the sins: forgotten. The verse displays the pardon: complete. The new covenant promise of mercy and forgotten sins displays the foundation of all its blessings: God will be merciful to their unrighteousness and remember their sins no more — a full and final forgiveness, the root from which the inward law and the universal knowledge of God spring. The verse displays the forgiving new covenant, which makes the first old (v.13).
13:
"In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away." The witnesses note the old covenant made obsolete and ready to vanish. On "in that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old," the grammatical voices note the implication. Barnes and Robertson note the writer's inference from the word "new": "in that he saith, A new (kaine) covenant, he hath made (declared, treated as) the first old (palaios — antiquated, obsolete)" — the very calling of one covenant "new" implies that the other is old, superseded; God's word in Jeremiah pronounced the old covenant obsolete.

On "that which decayeth and waxeth old," the witnesses note the aging. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: "that which decayeth (is becoming obsolete) and waxeth old (grows aged, like a worn garment)" — the old covenant, declared old, is in process of passing away; what is aged is near its end.

On "is ready to vanish away," the grammatical-historical note the imminent end: it "is ready (near) to vanish away (disappear, be abolished)" — the old covenant is on the verge of disappearing; written before AD 70, this may hint at the coming destruction of the temple and the final passing of the old order. The verse displays the old covenant made obsolete and ready to vanish.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — "in that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old (obsolete)," the word "new" implying the other is superseded, God pronouncing the old obsolete (Barnes, Robertson); "that which decayeth and waxeth old," the old covenant aging like a worn garment, in process of passing (Barnes, Calvin); "is ready to vanish away," near its disappearance, perhaps hinting at the temple's coming destruction (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vanishing old covenant. The verse marks the implication: new makes the first old. The verse displays the aging: decayeth and waxeth old. The verse displays the nearness: ready to vanish. The verse displays the verdict: obsolete. The inference displays the old covenant superseded and passing. The verse displays the new: announced. The verse displays the old: obsolete. The verse displays the vanishing: imminent. The old covenant made obsolete and ready to vanish displays the conclusion of the chapter: by calling one covenant "new," God declared the first old — and what is aged and decaying is near to vanishing away; the old order, superseded by the new, is on the verge of passing forever. The verse displays the obsolete old covenant, whose earthly sanctuary is now described (ch.9).
